qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Authenticated low-privilege users can disable auth and unlock admin-only routes
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding targets authorization logic around `PUT /api/system-config` and downstream `requireAdmin()` checks in user-management routes. The advisory targets unsanitized `manifest.name` in `POST /api/mcpb/upload` reaching path construction and filesystem operations in `src/controllers/mcpbController.ts`, which is a different vulnerable code path and root cause.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding in this session identifies the advisory's MCPB manifest-name path traversal root cause in `uploadMcpbFile`.
evidence: GHSA-p3h2-2j4p-p83g describes a path traversal in `POST /api/mcpb/upload` where `manifest.name` is concatenated into the final extraction path and reused by cleanup logic in `src/controllers/mcpbController.ts`; the fixing commit adds `validateMcpbServerName()` and `resolveMcpbServerExtractDir()` before those filesystem operations.

The only final qualifying finding materialized by this session is `authenticated_system_config_auth_bypass`, whose report and PoC target `PUT /api/system-config` in `src/controllers/serverController.ts` and the resulting bypass of `requireAdmin()` in `src/controllers/userController.ts`.

That qualifying finding does not identify attacker-controlled `manifest.json` input, MCPB upload handling, path joining for `server-${manifest.name}`, arbitrary filesystem write/delete outside the intended upload directory, or any equivalent source-to-sink condition.

`artifacts/component_family_checklist.md` separately notes the MCPB upload family and describes `manifest.name` path escape as a pruned sibling hypothesis rather than the accepted qualifying finding, which confirms the session did not finalize the advisory root cause as a qualifying report.
